abstract: Multivariate time series (MTS) forecasting is crucial for decision-making in domains such as weather, energy, and finance. It remains challenging because real-world sequences intertwine slow trends, multi-rate seasonalities, and irregular residuals. Existing methods often rely on rigid, hand-crafted decompositions or generic end-to-end architectures that entangle components and underuse structure shared across variables. To address these limitations, we propose DecompSSM, an end-to-end decomposition framework using three parallel deep state space model branches to capture trend, seasonal, and residual components. The model features adaptive temporal scales via an input-dependent predictor, a refinement module for shared cross-variable context, and an auxiliary loss that enforces reconstruction and orthogonality. Across standard benchmarks (ECL, Weather, ETTm2, and PEMS04), DecompSSM outperformed strong baselines, indicating the effectiveness of combining component-wise deep state space models and global context refinement.

title: Robust Federated Fine-Tuning Over Heterogeneous and Unreliable Communication Networks
authors: Yanmeng Wang, Zhiwen Dai, Jian Zhou, Fu Xiao, Tsung-Hui Chang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Federated fine-tuning (FFT) has attracted growing attention as it leverages both server- and client-side data resources to improve global model generalization while preserving privacy. However, its performance is often hindered by unreliable server-client connections and heterogeneous client data. Existing strategies typically assume uniform network conditions or require prior knowledge of connection failures, which is impractical in real-world networks with diverse network standards and heterogeneous failure types. To address this, we propose FedAuto, a novel FFT scheme that mitigates the impact of connection failures through adaptive aggregation, without requiring prior knowledge of network conditions or infrastructure modifications, enabling seamless plug-and-play deployment. We also establish rigorous convergence guarantees for FedAuto. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FedAuto consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines under diverse connection failure scenarios and even surpasses strategies that rely on complex resource optimization.

title: Decision Fusedconv: Efficient Offline Reinforcement Learning Via Fused State-Reward Encoding and Hybrid Temporal Convolution
authors: Zhuojing Tian, Yushu Chen, Haoyu Ma, Wenlai Zhao, Zhao Liu, Hongkun Yu, Wusheng Zhang, Shiming Song, Dianxi Shi
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Decision Transformer (DT) represents return-to-go, state, and action as independent tokens, resulting in inflated sequence length and quadratic attention cost. To address this inefficiency, we propose Decision FusedConv (DFC), which jointly encodes return and state to shorten sequences and employs a gated hybrid convolutional module that integrates global uniform and local heterogeneous convolutions. This design effectively captures both long-range and modality-specific temporal dependencies while avoiding the overhead of self-attention. Experiments on MuJoCo and navigation benchmarks show that DFC consistently outperforms DT and recent variants, achieving up to a 64.9% reduction in FLOPs compared to DT, making it particularly suitable for resource-constrained settings. Our code is available at: https://github.com/Drama1234/Decision-FusedConv

title: Experimental Evaluation of Synthetic GAN based Data Augmentation Scheme to Predict Improved Rare Diseases from Medical Imaging
authors: L Pattathurani, Shanthashalini, Yashashwini S, S Priya, Soumya T R, M Arun
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: Medical imaging can pose a vital challenge to rare disease detection because of the scarcity of medical imaging data and imbalance in the classes. This paper presents a synthetic augmentation framework, Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (cGAN), which can be used to solve these problems and enhance the work of diagnostic systems in various categories of rare diseases. Open datasets of MRI, CT, and X-ray modalities that were obtained and published in Kaggle were curated, and annotated classes of muscular dystrophies, rare brain lesions, and pediatric skeletal anomalies. It implemented a piping system that consists of preprocessing, synthetic image generation via GANs and the downstream CNN classifier (ResNet50, DenseNet121, EfficientNet). The outcomes prove that GAN-enhanced datasets outperform in classification and recall significantly. In particular, the ResNet50 has an accuracy of 91.4 that is higher compared to real-only (84.1) and classical augmented (86.9) models. Optimal performance was also observed at a synthetic to real ratio of 5: 1 as found out in ablation studies. The FID (36.3), Inception Score (3.78), and SSIM (0.76) quantitative quality measurements were used to determine the realism and diversity of synthetic samples. The article demonstrates the potential of synthetic augmentation to enhance the ability to detect underrepresented pathologies and offers a repeatable design of improving rare disease classifiers. The approach can be applied in the medical fields with limited labeled data but early diagnosis is a critical factor.

title: CorrEctor: An Execute-to-Correct Paradigm for Efficient LLM Secure Inference
authors: Dong Liu, Bingzheng Wang, Yifan Zeng, Minghui Pan, Jiang Zhou, Xiaoyan Gu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) provide hardware-level isolation that protects sensitive user data during Large Language Model (LLM) inference. However, mainstream CPU-based TEEs lack the capability to perform largescale matrix operations efficiently. To mitigate this, many approaches offload computations to untrusted GPUs via parameter encryption or differential privacy, but these typically introduce substantial communication overhead or accuracy degradation. To achieve efficient LLM inference while protecting user request privacy without compromising accuracy, we propose an execute-to-correct paradigm for efficient LLM secure inference, namely CorrEctor. The core mechanism of CorrEctor involves generating a sequence of candidate tokens from user inputs protected by differential privacy on untrusted GPUs, followed by verification and correction within a TEE. Experimental results on mainstream LLMs show that CorrEctor outperforms CPU-based TEE baselines by 1.36× to 3.55× in inference acceleration.

title: Alignment-Aware Unrolling Network for Multimodal MRI Reconstruction
authors: Yi Du, Tingting Wang, Ziyi Wang, Sunzhe Yang, Faming Fang, Guixu Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Deep unrolling networks are effective for multimodal MRI reconstruction but struggle to capture cross-modal relationships, limiting reference modality utilization. Moreover, patient motion may induce spatial misalignments, exacerbating the reconstruction difficulty. To address these challenges, we propose an Alignment-aware Unrolling Network (AUNet) that employs semi-implicit cross-modal modeling to capture structural correlations between target and reference images. Specifically, we utilize convolutional sparse coding (CSC) with a shared dictionary to jointly represent structural features of both modalities in the aligned latent space. Furthermore, we introduce a Bidirectional Deformable Sampler (BDS) within the unrolling stages to accurately estimate cross-modal displacement, thereby mitigating spatial misalignment and enhancing reference feature extraction. Our experimental results show that AUNet outperforms existing methods, effectively handling misalignment and improving reconstruction quality. The complete code has been made publicly available at: https://github.com/Corsilent/AUNet.

title: Place Anywhere: Learning Spatial Reasoning for Occlusion-Aware Image Composition
authors: Zan Li, Bingnan Wang, Wencheng Wang, Fei Hou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Image composition aims to seamlessly blend objects into new environments, which is a fundamental task in computer vision and graphics. Existing methods often cannot fully understand the 3D contents. They only allow to insert an object into part of the image, where there are no occlusion issues. To address this limitation, occlusions between foreground and background objects must be carefully identified to freely generate a reasonable composited image. We propose Place Anywhere, a framework with strong spatial reasoning capability that learns to resolve occlusion relationships in arbitrary compositions. Given a foreground object and a background scene, our model predicts a visibility mask for the foreground, indicating which regions should remain visible after considering occlusion. This is achieved through our carefully designed feature extraction, fusion, and depth-guided modules, along with a novel method for constructing training data that incorporates spatial relationships. Experimental results show that Place Anywhere can generalize well, learn robust 3D spatial reasoning, and produce realistic compositions without explicit placement constraints. The source code will be made publicly available at https://github.com/Zannnn/autoComposition.

title: PMW-Dehaze: Multi-Scale Wavelet Fusion for Image Dehazing via Mamba Framework
authors: Jiayi Chen, Zhuang Zhou, Xin Zhang, Wenyao Qiu, Haijun Xie, Binghua Su, Ran Tao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Single image dehazing remains highly challenging due to the ill-posed nature of the atmospheric scattering model and the limited capability of existing methods to capture long-range dependencies. To address these issues, we propose a novel lightweight framework, termed PMW-Dehaze. Specifically, we design an atmospheric scattering network (ASN) to explicitly model the haze formation process, followed by progressive refinement within the PMW-Dehaze pipeline to achieve stepwise dehazing. Furthermore, we introduce a U-shaped Wavelet-UMamba Block that integrates multi-scale wavelet decomposition with a multi-view Mamba module: the former enhances feature representation through decomposition and multi-scale fusion, while the latter strengthens long-range dependency modeling via an efficient state space mechanism and effectively alleviates the blocking artifact problem that is easily caused by conventional single-patch partitioning through multi-view tokens. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PMW-Dehaze achieves superior generalization across diverse hazy scenarios while significantly reducing computational cost.

title: Boot-and-Feedback Framework for Generalist-Expert Model Collaboration in Breast Ultrasound Diagnosis
authors: Ming Cheng, Hongyu Sun, Zhaolin Chen, Jun Liu, Hossein Rahmani, Qiuhong Ke
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Breast ultrasound (BUS) is widely used for breast cancer diagnosis yet remains operator-dependent. While deep learning shows promise, ensuring diagnostic reliability and interpretability is challenging. Recent Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) often generate spurious descriptions due to limited domain knowledge, which mislead downstream expert models and compromise clinical validity. To address these challenges, we propose the Boot-and-Feedback (BooF) model collaboration framework for synergistic MLLM-expert interaction. Specifically, in the Boot Stage, the MLLM is guided by the BI-RADS lexicon and preliminary benign-malignant vision-expert predictions, enabling it to transfer general reasoning to BUS analysis while avoiding hallucinations. Subsequently, the Feedback Stage integrates these descriptions with visual features via a lightweight Attention-Gated Cross-Modality Fusion Module. This allows the expert to leverage textual feedback while adaptively filtering noise. Extensive experiments on multiple BUS datasets demonstrate that BooF substantially outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of diagnostic accuracy and interpretability.

title: EDN-Gaussian: Edge-Directed Densification with Covariance Narrowing For Blur-Robust 3d Gaussian Splatting
authors: Tianzhen Dong, Junjie Wang, Wei He, Cong Lin, Youkang Tu, Chenyang Xu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) can achieve efficient novel view synthesis but relies on point cloud initialization using Structure-from-Motion (SfM) or Multi-View Stereo (MVS). Due to motion blur, de-focus, or poor illumination, the reconstructed clouds are often sparse and noisy, leading to limited coverage and unstable convergence. In light of these observations, we propose the EDN-Gaussian method, which enhances visibility via joint covariance–opacity adjustment. It employs edge-directed adaptive densification with anisotropic narrowing and growth-rate control, and introduces a split regularizer to constrain the newborn Gaussians by their parent Gaussians.Vast experiments on the GS-Blur and Deblur-NeRF datasets demonstrate significant improvements in geometric accuracy and rendering quality with degraded inputs.

title: LumiDiff: Luminance-Prior Guided Diffusion for Perceptually Balanced Low-Light Image Signal Recovery
authors: Wenwei Luo, Xiangxu Zhao, Zhikun Zheng, Mengmeng Jing, Lin Zuo
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Low-light image recovery can be formulated as a two-dimensional signal restoration problem corrupted by non-stationary degradation factors. To recover the image signal, a critical challenge is the non-uniform illumination caused by non-stationary lighting conditions. Traditional signal processing methods often introduce artifacts during recovery. In this paper, we propose Luminance-Prior Guided Diffusion (LumiDiff) to alleviate this problem. Specifically, we construct a luminance distribution map as luminance prior to capture the light distribution in degraded images, ensuring accurate representation of illumination variances. Subsequently, we utilize the luminance distribution map to guide the global-local information aggregation, effectively restoring areas of extreme darkness and reducing the uneven illumination effects. Finally, we utilize the inherent noise modeling capability of diffusion models to perform overall luminance correction while simultaneously removing the amplified noise from the enhancement process. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework achieves superior balance of quantitative metrics and visual quality compared to existing methods.

title: SIREN: Spatially-Informed Reconstruction of Binaural Audio with Vision
authors: Mingyeong Song, Seoyeon Ko, Junhyug Noh
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Binaural audio delivers spatial cues essential for immersion, yet most consumer videos are monaural due to capture constraints. We introduce SIREN, a visually guided mono→binaural framework that explicitly predicts left and right channels. A ViT-based encoder learns dual-head self-attention to produce a shared scene map and end-to-end L/R attention – replacing hand-crafted masks. A soft, annealed spatial prior gently biases early L/R grounding, and a two-stage, confidence-weighted waveform-domain fusion (guided by mono reconstruction and interaural phase consistency) suppresses crosstalk when aggregating multi-crop and overlapping windows. Evaluated on FAIR-Play and MUSIC-Stereo, SIREN yields consistent gains on time–frequency and phase-sensitive metrics with competitive SNR. The design is modular and generic, requires no task-specific annotations, and integrates with standard audio-visual pipelines.

title: Improving Continuous Sign Language Recognition via Lightweight Adaptive Temporal Mixing
authors: Yining Jiang, Sheng Liu, Yuan Feng, Min Xu, Yiheng Yu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Current continuous sign language recognition (CSLR) methods either overlook the dynamics of cross-frame motions or rely on hand-crafted and complex strategies, leading to computational overhead and limited adaptability. To address these issues, we propose DMSign, which integrates two complementary modules: Dynamic Patch Mixing (DPM) and Dynamic Channel Mixing (DCM). DPM reorganizes local spatial patches across frames to flexibly model subtle sign variations, while DCM applies a learnable per-channel temporal filter that dynamically allocates information across frames, thereby promoting complementary feature fusion. Both modules are lightweight and adaptive, balancing efficiency and computational cost. DMSign achieves a new state-of-the-art on three large-scale datasets, i.e., PHOENIX14, PHOENIX14-T, and CSL-Daily.

title: RTNLW: Reversible and Tamper-Aware Natural Language Watermarking Scheme for Multi-Stage Transmission
authors: Tianyi Chen, Bin Liu, Weihai Li, Zikai Xu, Wanying Xu, Xiang Zhang, Nenghai Yu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In multi-stage data transmission scenarios, watermarking plays a crucial role in data traceability. However, highly sensitive applications also impose strict requirements on textual integrity. Existing natural language watermarking schemes often disrupt semantic coherence, resulting in irreversible distortion and preventing effective integrity verification. To address these limitations, we propose a Reversible and Tamper-aware Natural Language Watermarking (RTNLW) scheme that leverages a masked language model and Prediction Error Expansion (PEE). The scheme enables watermark extraction while restoring the original text. In addition, the expansion values of PEE enable tampering detection prior to text integrity verification. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RTNLW achieves a high watermark embedding capacity while preserving semantics effectively. Moreover, the proposed scheme is robust against text tampering attacks, maintaining a bit recognition rate above 85% under a modification rate of 5%.

title: Active Inference Framework for Closed-Loop Sensing, Communication, and Control in UAV Systems
authors: Guangjin Pan, Liping Bai, Zhuojun Tian, Hui Chen, Mehdi Bennis, Henk Wymeersch
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Integrated sensing and communication is a core technology for 6G, and its application to closed-loop sensing, communication, and control (SCC) enables various services. Existing SCC solutions often treat sensing and control separately, leading to suboptimal performance and resource usage. In this work, we introduce the active inference framework into SCC-enabled uncrewed aerial vehicle systems for joint state estimation, control, and sensing resource allocation. By formulating a unified generative model, the problem reduces to minimizing variational free energy for inference and expected free energy for action planning. Simulation results show that both control cost and sensing cost are reduced relative to baselines.

title: Learning Dual Mixture-of-Experts Models for Unified Image Deraining
authors: Tianyi Yang, Mengke Zhang, Tianshuo Chen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Despite the remarkable progress achieved in image deraining, most existing approaches remain constrained to handling a single type of rain degradation or a specific rain pattern. In practice, however, rain manifests in diverse forms, which severely limits the applicability of current methods under real-world conditions. To overcome this limitation, we first construct a high-quality benchmark named Hybrid-Rain10K, which integrates three representative rain degradation patterns, including rain streaks, raindrops and rain accumulation. Building upon this benchmark, we propose a dual mixture-of-experts (MoE) model for unified image deraining, which adaptively handles various rain degradations and restores clean images. Where the encoder employs a Soft-MoE to assign collaborative weights across all experts, ensuring comprehensive degradation modeling, while the decoder adopts a Hard-MoE with top-k routing guided by high-frequency priors to enhance reconstruction quality. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our dataset and model.

title: BHSFlow: Low-Latency Flow Estimation with Block-Wise Huber Loss and Simplified Structure
authors: Tianyi Sun, Yanze Wang, Runkai Chen, Yuedi Chen, Jun Sun
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Optical flow estimation provides motion information between two frames, acting as a key component in many visual tasks. Current methods either come with large computational complexity or suffer a severe accuracy drop to achieve real-time performance, especially when dealing with high-resolution frames. To address this trade-off, this paper proposes a low-latency recurrent pyramid network structure and a novel block-wise Huber loss applicable to all pyramid optical flow networks. By characterizing latency and FLOPs of various convolutional kernels, a fast yet effective pyramid structure is designed accordingly. Standard Huber loss mitigates the impact of small errors but risks allowing large errors to disproportionately influence the loss within a block in certain scenarios. Instead, our block-wise Huber loss first balances opposing errors within each block and selectively applies Huber loss only to the residual errors, ensuring stable optimization. Experiments show that our method achieves the best result among existing lightweight methods and demonstrates strong adaptability to frames in high resolution. Our code is available in https://github.com/cppppp/BHSFlow.

title: Enhancing Dialogue-Related Speech Tasks with Generated Spoken Dialogues
authors: Haitian Lu, Zhihao Bai, Yukun Liu, Xuyang Wang, Gaofeng Cheng, Yonghong Yan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: With the rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs), speech language models (SLMs) have enabled more natural spoken dialogue generation. However, to make generated dialogues useful for downstream tasks, key challenges remain in ensuring consistency between text and speech as well as providing accurate utterance timestamps. Building upon the SLIDE framework, this work introduces a method for generating dual-channel spoken dialogues with aligned transcriptions and utterance timestamps, and systematically investigates their effectiveness as augmentation data. In particular, we explore how such generated dialogues can be leveraged for downstream training in a task-specific manner—either alone or in combination with real data—across automatic speech recognition, end-to-end neural diarization, voice activity detection, and overlap speech detection. Extensive experiments on the Fisher and CALLHOME corpora demonstrate that our approach yields substantial performance gains across tasks, highlighting its effectiveness and practical value for speech processing applications.

title: MoRE: Multimodal Relationship Enhancement for Unbiased Scene Graph Generation
authors: Ruiqi Hu, Lida Huang, Bin Jiang, Chao Yang, Jianbo Zheng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Scene Graph Generation (SGG) extracts relational triplets 〈subject, predicate, object〉 from images, yet current benchmarks suffer from long-tailed distributions and semantic ambiguity, causing biased predictions toward generic relations (e.g., "on") and neglecting fine-grained ones (e.g., "parked on"). Existing balancing strategies reallocate predicates but largely ignore contextual semantics and underexploit missing relations. We propose Multimodal Relationship Enhancement (MoRE), a framework that improves relation prediction by addressing contextual inconsistency and annotation sparsity. MoRE introduces a Context-relation Bank to dynamically retrieve candidate predicates, and employs Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) with context-driven prompts for semantically consistent relation selection. Experiments on VG and GQA demonstrate that MoRE achieves state-of-the-art performance on unbiased metrics, enhancing predicate diversity and prediction balance for SGG.

title: Large Vision Models can Solve Mental Rotation Problems
authors: Sebastian Ray Mason, Anders Gjølbye, Phillip Chavarria Højbjerg, Lenka Tětková, Lars Kai Hansen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Mental rotation is a key test of spatial reasoning in humans and has been central to understanding how perception supports cognition. Despite the success of modern vision transformers, it is still unclear how well these models develop similar abilities. In this work, we present a systematic evaluation of ViT, CLIP, DINOv2, and DINOv3 across a range of mental-rotation tasks, from simple block structures similar to those used by Shepard and Metzler to study human cognition, to more complex block figures, three types of text, and photo-realistic objects. By probing model representations layer by layer, we examine where and how these networks succeed. We find that i) self-supervised ViTs capture geometric structure better than supervised ViTs; ii) intermediate layers perform better than final layers; iii) task difficulty increases with rotation complexity and occlusion, mirroring human reaction times and suggesting similar constraints in embedding space representations.

title: ViTex: Visual Texture Control for Multi-Track Symbolic Music Generation via Discrete Diffusion Models
authors: Xiaoyu Yi, Qi He, Gus Xia, Ziyu Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In automatic music generation, a central challenge is to design controls that enable meaningful human-machine interaction. Existing systems often rely on extrinsic inputs such as text prompts or metadata, which do not allow humans to directly shape the composition. While prior work has explored intrinsic controls such as chords or hierarchical structure, these approaches mainly address piano or vocal-accompaniment settings, leaving multitrack symbolic music largely underexplored. We identify instrumentation, the choice of instruments and their roles, as a natural dimension of control in multi-track composition, and propose ViTex, a visual representation of instrumental texture. In ViTex, color encodes instrument choice, spatial position represents pitch and time, and stroke properties capture local textures. Building on this representation, we develop a discrete diffusion model conditioned on ViTex and chord progressions to generate 8-measure multi-track symbolic music, enabling explicit texture-level control while maintaining strong unconditional generation quality. The demo page and code are avaliable at https://vitex2025.github.io/.

title: Deep Spatial Clue Informed Ambisonic Encoding for Irregular Microphone Arrays
authors: Chaoqun Zhuang, Xue Wen, Lin Ma, Lizhong Wang, Liang Wen, Jaehyun Kim, Gangyoul Kim
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Ambisonic encoding from irregular microphone arrays is challenging due to spatial aliasing and limited coverage of the sound field. Existing deep learning approaches typically operate on fixed time–frequency representations (e.g., STFT), which restricts the network’s ability to fully exploit high level spatial correlations. In this work, we propose a Deep Spatial Clue Informed Ambisonic Encoder, a fully end-to-end deep neural network (DNN) that estimates a nonlinear transformation in a learned latent space. Explicit spatial clues (IPD, ILD) are fused with deep spectral features to guide the model toward physically consistent mappings. Unlike prior work, the transformation is applied to latent feature maps instead of shallow spectra, enabling adaptive representation learning and more accurate reconstruction. Experiments on real commercial mobile phone microphone arrays in multi-source reverberant scenes demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms least squares and recent neural ambisonic encoders across spatial similarity, spectral error, and SI-SDR metrics, while requiring fewer parameters than competing neural methods.

title: Hierarchical Sparse Vector Transmission for Ultra Reliable and Low Latency Communications
authors: Yanfeng Zhang, Xi’An Fan, Jinkai Zheng, Xiaoye Jing, Weiwei Yang, Xu Zhu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Sparse vector transmission (SVT) is a promising candidate technology for achieving ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC). In this paper, a hierarchical SVT scheme is proposed for multi-user URLLC scenarios. The hierarchical SVT scheme partitions the transmitted bits into common and private parts. The common information is conveyed by the indices of non-zero sections in a sparse vector, while each user’s private information is embedded into non-zero blocks with specific block lengths. At the receiver, the common bits are first recovered from the detected non-zero sections, followed by user-specific private bits decoding based on the corresponding non-zero block indices. Simulation results show the proposed scheme outperforms state-of-the-art SVT schemes in terms of block error rate.

title: Dissecting Performance Degradation in Audio Source Separation under Sampling Frequency Mismatch
authors: Kanami Imamura, Tomohiko Nakamura, Kohei Yatabe, Hiroshi Saruwatari
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Audio processing methods based on deep neural networks are typically trained at a single sampling frequency (SF). To handle untrained SFs, signal resampling of the input signal is commonly employed, but it can degrade performance, particularly when the input SF is lower than the trained SF. This paper investigates the causes of this degradation through two hypotheses: (i) the lack of high-frequency components introduced by up-sampling, and (ii) the greater importance of their presence than their spectral contents. To examine these hypotheses, we compare conventional resampling with three alternatives: post-resampling noise addition, which adds Gaussian noise to the resampled signal; noisy-kernel resampling, which perturbs the kernel with Gaussian noise to enrich high-frequency components; and trainable-kernel resampling, which adapts the interpolation kernel through training. Experiments on music source separation show that noisy-kernel and trainable-kernel resampling alleviate the degradation observed with conventional resampling. We further demonstrate that noisy-kernel resampling is effective across diverse models, highlighting it as a simple yet practical option to alleviate the performance degradation.

title: AIMRec: Aligning both Individuals and Modalities for Multimodal Recommendation
authors: Yihan Zhang, Ercan E. Kuruoglu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multimodal recommendation requires learning expressive representations of users and items by integrating multimodal features such as text and images. While prior studies have extensively explored graph convolution-based frameworks and modality fusion strategies, a critical issue that remains underexplored is the alignment of representations across embedding spaces. In this work, we propose a simple yet effective model, AIMRec, which performs graph convolution separately on ID and feature embeddings before combining them into unified user and item representations. To enhance consistency between different representations, we introduce two auxiliary alignment losses: an ID–Feature Alignment loss that enforces coherence between ID and feature embeddings of individuals, and a Cross-Modal Alignment loss that aligns item representations across modalities from both local and global perspectives. Extensive experiments on three real-world datasets demonstrate the superiority of AIMRec over baselines. Further ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of each proposed component.

title: MHAN: A Multi-Module Hybrid Attention Network for Robust Classification of Appendiceal Cancer and Appendicitis
authors: Rengmin Wu, Dan Liang, Jiaqiang Li, Yongtao Zhang, Wenzheng Hu, Hui Zhou, Guangming Li, Xinhua Wei, Tianfu Wang, Baiying Lei
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Appendicitis is a common gastrointestinal disease, but appendiceal cancer is a rare malignancy. The similarities between the two diseases often lead to missed diagnosis in clinical practice, resulting in serious consequences. To address this problem, we propose a multi-module hybrid attention network (MHAN) to tackle the challenge of classifying appendiceal cancer and appendicitis in CT images. Our framework comprises four innovative modules. The multi-level feature extraction (MFE) enhances the multi-scale representation of small lesions. The hypergraph-based feature interaction (HFI) captures high-order cross-layer dependencies. The agent attention fusion (AAF) supports cross-layer recursive semantically guided enhancement. Finally, a reinforcement learning-based Dynamic loss optimization (RDLO) improves robustness in imbalanced data problem. We validate the proposed model on both internal and external datasets, and the results demonstrate significant improvements in lesion representation and diagnostic performance.

title: Precision Matters: Comparing Deep Models for Visual Marker Detection on Eyewear-Mounted Calibration Objects
authors: Adrian-Pavel Măduța
publish: 2025 27th International Symposium on Symbolic and Numeric Algorithms for Scientific Computing (SYNASC)
abstract: This paper addresses the problem of localizing hazard-shaped calibration markers mounted on eyeglass frames, a task relevant to augmented reality, head pose estimation and optical distance calculations. The goal is to accurately detect these markers, which lie in the upper portion of the face, within a rectangular region provided by a face detector. We revisit this problem and ask: how much precision can be reliably extracted, and at what computational cost? Beyond a controlled comparison of heatmap-based segmentation models (HRNet modified for multiscale outputs, MobileNetV3) and object detection with YOLOv8 variants, we introduce two contributions that improve accuracy while retaining real-time performance: (i) a temperature-scaled soft-argmax readout for subpixel localization with calibrated spatial confidence, and (ii) a lightweight Detection → Refinement cascade that couples YOLO proposals with a 7-layer heatmap refiner operating on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$64 \times 64$</tex> crops. Experiments are conducted on both synthetic and real datasets spanning diverse capture distances. Our cascade reduces mean pixel error by 17 % over YOLOv8-n while running at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 8 ~ m s}$</tex> per frame (includes additional latency such as image loading, resizing, and patch extraction, inference time alone is 75 ms per frame), and outperforms HRNet by 4.7% AUC@2px at a fraction of its latency. Evaluation includes distance-based error metrics, uncertainty calibration (ECE), and both quantitative and qualitative analyses. Results show a strong correlation between confidence and localization error, enabling practical deployment.

title: ActiveParam: Selective Parameterization for Efficient and Robust Retrieval-Augmented Generation
authors: Siqiang Li, Xifan Liu, Peng Peng, Shouqiang Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) enhances Large Language Models (LLMs) by incorporating external knowledge. However, parametric RAG (PRAG) methods often suffer from redundancy and noise sensitivity, leading to inefficiency and degraded robustness. We propose ActiveParam, a framework for selective knowledge parameterization. ActiveParam introduces a Knowledge Probing stage, where the LLM computes an ignorance score to identify knowledge gaps for each document. Only documents with high scores, indicating novel information, are parameterized through lightweight adapters. Experiments on four question-answering benchmarks and three LLMs show that ActiveParam consistently improves F1 scores by 2-5 points over baselines like Naive Combine, while simultaneously reducing the number of required parameter adapters. This work thus establishes that selective parameterization, guided by our novel ignorance score, offers a more efficient and robust path for knowledge augmentation.

title: Research on Collaborative Interference Resource Allocation Based on Improved Genetic Algorithm
authors: Pengjiao Li, Haoran Gong, Yinshuai Meng, Yumeng Liang, Keding Yan
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Automation, Robotics and Applications (ICARA)
abstract: In the process of unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) formation penetration, when multiple interference aircraft jointly suppress multiple radars, there are problems such as limited resources, high decision-making dimensions, and low optimization efficiency. This paper constructs a collaborative interference resource allocation model with the goal of maximizing interference effectiveness, comprehensively considering the power and time coordination mechanism as well as the constraints of interference targets and styles. On this basis, an improved genetic algorithm is proposed. By introducing sorting selection, small habitat elimination, and elite retention strategies, the algorithm's global search ability is enhanced and the convergence speed is accelerated. The results show that the proposed algorithm can converge to a better solution within approximately 60% of the iterations, significantly reducing the probability of enemy radar detection, increasing the positioning and tracking errors by 2 to 3 times, effectively compressing the radar exposure area, and providing a relatively safe flight corridor for UAV penetration.

title: BDRNet:Bidirectional Decomposed and Recalibrated Lightweight Network for Human Pose Estimation
authors: Jing Luo, Yanxia Wang, Xuping Liang, Wenwen Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Lightweight human pose estimation models often struggle to balance accuracy and efficiency in complex scenarios. Their limited receptive fields and feature representation capabilities lead to significant performance degradation under occlusions, crowded environments, etc. Existing methods typically address this issue by introducing multi-scale modules or global attention mechanisms, which often incur substantial computational costs or fail to effectively model the balance between long-range dependencies and local details. To alleviate this problem, this study proposes a lightweight cross-scale attention fusion network named BDRNet. The network innovatively integrates two key modules: the Bidirectional Decomposed Spatial Attention (BDSA) and the Multi-Scale Recalibrated Attention (MSRA). BDSA captures directional local details with minimal computational cost, while MSRA further efficiently models global contextual relationships and integrates local details through the Recalibrated Hybrid Attention (RHA) and Pointwise Shuffle Block (PWSB). Experimental results on the MSCOCO and MPII datasets demonstrate that BDRNet achieves competitive overall performance, excels in complex scenarios, approaches the performance of large-scale network models on average precision (AP), and outperforms most other lightweight models, striking a favorable balance between accuracy and efficiency.

title: Unsupervised Landslide Detection from InSAR-Derived Time Series Using Change Point Analysis
authors: Ioana-Cristina Igret, Gabriel Iuhasz, Delia Teleagă, Valentin Poncos
publish: 2025 27th International Symposium on Symbolic and Numeric Algorithms for Scientific Computing (SYNASC)
abstract: Landslides pose dangerous risks to human safety and to infrastructure, especially in densely populated or geologically unpredictable regions. Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR) technology, along with time series analysis has significantly improved the ability to monitor ground deformations associated with such hazards. This study explores the detection of change points in InSAR-derived ground motion time series from the Praid region in Romania, based on Sentinel-1 data collected over a period of five years. The methodology starts with clustering Persistent Scatterers (PS) with similar deformation patterns using the HDBSCAN algorithm, followed by the application of several detrending methods to enhance change point detection accuracy. Four algorithms, Pruned Exact Linear Time (PELT), Binary Segmentation (BS), Window Sliding and Bottom-Up Segmentation, are implemented to identify abrupt fluctuations in ground motion. The absence of ground truth data motivated the selection of a consensus-based on majority voting as evaluation system. More specifically, the validation of a detected change point was correlated with the number of algorithms that identified it independently, with a minimum of two algorithms out of the four implemented. The findings offer insights into deformation activity and highlight the value of change point analysis in landslide detection. Future work will include a more comprehensive analysis of data particularities, the integration of contextual environmental factors and finally, the application of advanced time series techniques, to further enhance detection accuracy and improve early warning and risk management. Our code is available at https://github.com/ioanaigret/unsupervised-change-point-analysis-based-on-InSAR-derived-time-series.git

title: Gen3d:Generating Domain-Free 3D Scenes From a Single Image
authors: Yuxin Zhang, Ziyu Lu, Hongbo Duan, Keyu Fan, Pengting Luo, Peiyu Zhuang, Mengyu Yang, Houde Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Despite recent advancements in neural 3D reconstruction, the dependence on dense multi-view captures restricts their broader applicability.Additionally, 3D scene generation is vital for advancing embodied AI and world models, which depend on diverse, high-quality scenes for learning and evaluation.In this work, we propose Gen3d, a novel method for generation of high-quality, wide-scope, and generic 3D scenes from a single image. After the initial point cloud is created by lifting the RGBD image, Gen3d maintains and expands its world model.The 3D scene is finalized through optimizing a Gaussian splatting representation. Extensive experiments on diverse datasets demonstrate the strong generalization capability and superior performance of our method in generating a world model and Synthesizing high-fidelity and consistent novel views.

title: Enhance Balance between Generalization and Personalization for Vision-Language Models in Federated Learning
authors: Ziyun Cai, Yizhou Lu, Yawen Huang, Jie Song, Ye Liu, Xiao-Yuan Jing
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We propose FLORA, a novel Federated Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning framework that combines orthogonal low-rank adaptation for global prompt with attention-guided visual adapter to address the balance between generalization and personalization under data heterogeneity. Specifically, each client personalizes the global prompt through orthogonal low-rank adaptation term with an additional orthogonal loss, thereby achieving efficient local adaptation while maintaining the generalization of the global prompt. Furthermore, we design a lightweight attention-guided visual adapter for the image encoder to enhance cross-modal alignment, further balancing generalization and personalization. Extensive experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate that our FLORA achieves superior performance in balancing generalization and personalization over state-of-the-art methods.

title: Inverse Rendering for High-Genus 3D Surface Meshes from Multi-View Images with Persistent Homology Priors
authors: Xiang Gao, Xinmu Wang, Yuanpeng Liu, Yue Wang, Junqi Huang, Wei Chen, Xianfeng Gu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Reconstructing 3D objects from images is inherently an ill-posed problem due to ambiguities in geometry, appearance, and topology. This paper introduces collaborative inverse rendering with persistent homology priors, a novel strategy that leverages topological constraints to resolve these ambiguities. By incorporating priors that capture critical features such as tunnel loops and handle loops, our approach directly addresses the difficulty of reconstructing high-genus surfaces. The collaboration between photometric consistency from multi-view images and homology-based guidance enables recovery of complex high-genus geometry while circumventing catastrophic failures such as collapsing tunnels or losing high-genus structure. Instead of neural networks, our method relies on gradient-based optimization within a mesh-based inverse rendering framework to highlight the role of topological priors. Experimental results show that incorporating persistent homology priors leads to lower Chamfer Distance (CD) and higher Volume IoU compared to state-of-the-art mesh-based methods, demonstrating improved geometric accuracy and robustness against topological failure.

title: A Benchmark Dataset and Baseline Framework for Action Recognition in Power Construction Safety
authors: Shuo Zhang, Xiaoqing Zheng, Weibin Zhang, Yaguang Kong
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Unsafe behaviors of workers are a major cause of accidents in power engineering, while traditional manual inspections struggle to monitor complex, dynamic construction sites. Although video understanding has been widely applied to action recognition, power construction faces challenges such as large target scale variations, complex occlusions, and imbalanced class distributions. To address this, we introduce PCVA, a benchmark dataset for action recognition in power construction safety, and propose MSHCNet, a multi-scale spatio-temporal hierarchical collaborative network with an Adaptive Spatio-Temporal Hierarchical Module (ASHM) as a strong baseline. MSHCNet captures temporal dynamics and adaptively fuses multi-scale features for hierarchical action modeling. Experiments on PCVA show MSHCNet achieves 38.16 mAP, outperforming the previous best method by 7.54%, effectively supporting safety monitoring and accident prevention. The dataset and code are available at https://github.com/ys-sy/MSHCNet.

title: Syllable-Level Acoustic Modeling with a Stage-Aware Transformer for ALS Dysarthria Severity Estimation - ICASSP-2026 Sand Challenge
authors: Yuho Tamura, Mondher Bouazizi, Tomoaki Ohtsuki
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper presents a syllable-level Transformer-based model for estimating dysarthria severity in patients with Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) as part of the ICASSP-2026 SAND Challenge [1]. Our approach segments speech recordings into syllable-level acoustic units using Sylber and extracts eGeMAPS Low-Level Descriptors (LLDs). We then compute feature-level mutual information scores to rank and select the most discriminative features. After integrating features extracted from three distinct syllable recordings (/pa/, /ka/, and /ta/), we employ a stage-aware Transformer framework with separate models trained for each ALSFRS-R starting severity. Experimental results demonstrate that this syllable-level, stage-wise modeling improves the prediction of future ALS dysarthria severity.

title: Frontend Token Enhancement for Token-Based Speech Recognition
authors: Takanori Ashihara, Shota Horiguchi, Kohei Matsuura, Tsubasa Ochiai, Marc Delcroix
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Discretized representations of speech signals are efficient alternatives to continuous features for various speech applications, including automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech language models. However, these representations, such as semantic or phonetic tokens derived from clustering outputs of self-supervised learning (SSL) speech models, are susceptible to environmental noise, which can degrade backend task performance. In this work, we introduce a frontend system that estimates clean speech tokens from noisy speech and evaluate it on an ASR backend using semantic tokens. We consider four types of enhancement models based on their input/output domains: wave-to-wave, token-to-token, continuous SSL features-to-token, and wave-to-token. These models are trained independently of ASR backends. Experiments on the CHiME-4 dataset demonstrate that wave-to-token enhancement achieves the best performance among the frontends. Moreover, it mostly outperforms the ASR system based on continuous SSL features.

title: LLM4RIM: Leveraging Large Language Model for Radar-Based in-Vehicle Monitoring
authors: Chong Rao, Jiahang Guo, Xikang Jiang, Lei Li, Lin Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The growing need for non-invasive in-vehicle monitoring in the intelligent cabin underscores the potential of radar sensing. However, existing radar-based solutions often rely on task-specific approaches, lacking a unified framework across multiple downstream tasks. In this work, LLM4RIM, a large language model (LLM) multi-task fine-tuning framework is proposed for radar-based in-vehicle monitoring (RIM) tasks, including occupancy detection, drowsiness detection, gesture recognition, and vital sign monitoring. By utilizing the Mixture of Experts with Low-Rank Adaptation (MoE-LoRA) approach for fine-tuning, the framework leverages the cross-modal learning capabilities of LLMs to enable efficient multitask learning. Experiments conducted on four task-specific datasets demonstrate an average accuracy of 93.20%, outperforming the state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods by 13.48%. Our code is released at https://github.com/bupt-uwb/LLM4RIM.

title: Diversity is All You Need: Self-Supervised Hypergraph Learning for Mitigating Popularity Bias in Conversational Recommender System
authors: Yongsen Zheng, Ruilin Xu, Ye Ma, Guohua Wang, Liang Lin, Kwok-Yan Lam
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Popularity bias is the tendency of recommender systems (RSs) to favor popular items over personalized or niche options, reinforcing the popularity of well-liked items. Most existing methods aim to alleviate popularity bias in offline static environments, but this bias tends to intensify over time in online recommendations. To address these issues, we propose a novel framework, Self-Supervised Hypergraph Learning for Mitigating Popularity Bias in Conversational Recommender System (SHLCRS), which aims to reduce popularity bias in the dynamic user-system feedback loop by capturing diverse user preferences through hypergraphs. SHLCRS establishes a self-supervised learning paradigm to create paired hypergraphs and line graphs for exploring varied preferences. It then employs a Multi-Hypergraph-Oriented CRS to leverage these preferences for accurate item predictions and effective conversational responses. Extensive experiments on two public CRS benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of SHLCRS in mitigating popularity bias.

title: Radiance Field Rendering with Adaptive Compact Kernel for Novel View Synthesis
authors: Binghao Wu, Haiyang Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as a powerful explicit representation for novel view synthesis. However, its fixed Gaussian kernels introduce two key limitations: over-smoothed reconstructions due to limited adaptability, and truncation artifacts stemming from the kernel’s long-tailed and unbounded nature. To address these challenges, we present the Adaptive Compact Kernel (ACK), a shape-adaptive and boundary-aware representation that dynamically adjusts its spatial profile to capture both low-frequency structures and fine-grained details, while inherently eliminating truncation artifacts. In addition, we introduce a parameter compression scheme that reorganizes and quantizes primitives into compact PNG-based representations, achieving up to 82% storage reduction and 13% improvement in loading efficiency, with negligible degradation in rendering quality. Extensive experiments on four widely used benchmark datasets, including Mip-NeRF 360, Tanks and Temples, Deep Blending, and NeRF Synthetic, demonstrate that our method achieves superior efficiency and fidelity over prior state-of-the-art approaches.

title: Toward Embodied Intelligence: An Architecture for Natural Dialogue and Action Execution in Assistive Robots
authors: Karameldeen Omer, Andrea Monteriù
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Automation, Robotics and Applications (ICARA)
abstract: This work presents a new architecture for an assistive mobile robot designed to support the elderly and individuals with disabilities in performing daily indoor tasks. The proposed framework integrates multimodal perception, language-based reasoning, and safety-aware action planning to enable natural and effective two-way communication between humans and robots. At its core, the system utilizes large language models (LLMs) for dialogue management, contextual understanding, and reasoning over fused sensory inputs, including vision, speech, and proprioceptive data. By combining speech recognition, object detection, and local memory modules, the robot not only interprets explicit user commands but also infers implicit intentions, predicts missing information, and requests clarifications when necessary. A dedicated safety layer filters and validates action sequences before execution, ensuring reliability and user safety. The architecture further incorporates short- and long-term memory structures, enabling the robot to maintain a dialogue history and semantic knowledge of the environment. This bidirectional interaction model allows the robot to generate both natural conversational responses and executable action plans in a context-aware manner. Preliminary implementation and testing demonstrate promising performance, bridging the gap between conversational AI and embodied robotic action in real-life assistive scenarios.

title: From PowerSGD to PowerSGD+: Low-Rank Gradient Compression For Distributed Optimization With Convergence Guarantees
authors: Shengping Xie, Chuyan Chen, Kun Yuan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Low-rank gradient compression methods, such as PowerSGD, have gained attention in communication-efficient distributed optimization. However, the convergence guarantees of PowerSGD remain unclear, particularly in stochastic settings. In this paper, we show that PowerSGD does not always converge to the optimal solution and provide a clear counter-example to support this finding. To address this, we introduce PowerSGD+, which periodically updates the projection subspace via singular value decomposition, ensuring that it remains aligned with the optimal subspace. We prove that PowerSGD+ converges under standard assumptions and validate its effectiveness through empirical evaluation on large language model tasks.

title: Current-Tracking Technique Improves RF Amplifier Efficiency in Lower HF Frequency Band
authors: Aleksandar Kiričenko, Vedran Kos
publish: 2026 25th International Symposium INFOTEH-JAHORINA (INFOTEH)
abstract: Over the past decade, a prominent trend in mobile radio communications has been the increasing adoption of envelope-tracking techniques to enhance the efficiency of radio frequency (RF) power amplifiers. An envelope-tracking amplifier is a hybrid linear/non-linear circuit where the supply voltage to the linear amplifier is dynamically adjusted by a nonlinear (switched-mode) power supply circuit to match the instantaneous amplitude (envelope) of the input signal. Inspired by this principle of operation, the presented research investigates a more straightforward approach: feeding the antenna directly with current generated by a highly efficient switched-mode source. The viability of the principle has been verified by simulations and measurement results on a demonstrator in the lower HF frequency range. The results have confirmed the substantial efficiency increase, in the operating frequency range of non-linear circuit.

title: A Trustworthy Data-Driven Governance Framework for Ethical Decision Automation and Regulatory Compliance Management in Organizations
authors: Maha Lakshmi S, Nandini D, Priya R, Hubin Bens L, Thuseenth M, Vimalyabesh A
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: With the advent of smart automation, organizational governance systems are being under more pressure to make morally upright and legally correct decisions within dynamic environments. This paper gives a new Trustworthy Data-Driven Governance Framework that incorporates structural causal modeling (SCM), counterfactual reasoning, digital twin simulation, explainable reinforcement learning (XRL), Bayesian trust scoring, and federated auditable ledgers (FAL) in order to achieve transparency, accountability, and regulatory alignment. The model represents the policy effects on virtual simulations, selects the optimal decision paths through XRL, and automatically revises the trust scores of the data sources and service constituents. These experimental findings show a 97.5 prediction accuracy in the Ethics, convergence of the trust score in 6 iterations and 94 % compliance fidelity in the case of simulated governance. Also, the counterfactual engine successfully identified 60 % of ethically suboptimal decisions prior to implementation. The proposed model beats the traditional rule-based compliance engines in terms of ethical foresight, interpretability, and adaptiveness. The article provides a regulatory-conscious, scaled, and explainable governance infrastructure that can be used in areas like finance, healthcare, and government. The future work will introduce and include multi-jurisdictional alignment of regulatory ontology and real-time feedback of human stakeholders to further improve ethical negotiation in conflicting governance situations and assist in hybrid human-AI regulatory symbiosis.
